source: Portland Plant List – Nuisance Plants List & Required Eradication List
description: A selection of invasive weeds from the City of Portland's official Nuisance Plants List, organized into three threat tiers. Tier definitions are mapped from Portland's official ranking system: Silver = Rank B (widespread but patchier distribution), Gold = Rank A (spreading rapidly, limited but growing distribution), Platinum = Required Eradication List (legally mandated removal, highest public safety and environmental threat).

silver.label: Silver
silver.description: Rank B – Widespread and well-established invasive plants. Removal is strongly recommended but not legally required. Difficult to fully eradicate at city scale.
silver.legal_requirement: Prohibited from planting in Environmental Overlay Zones. Removal strongly encouraged.

gold.label: Gold
gold.description: Rank A – Spreading rapidly with currently limited distribution. Early detection and removal can prevent wider infestation. High environmental and public safety hazard.
gold.legal_requirement: Prohibited from planting. Active removal strongly encouraged; targeted by City management programs.

platinum.label: Platinum
platinum.description: Required Eradication List – The most dangerous invasive plants in Portland. Property owners are legally required to remove these species under Portland City Code Title 29.
platinum.legal_requirement: MANDATORY REMOVAL required by law for all private and public landowners. Free City assistance available.

plants.growth_habit: Sprawling thorny canes up to 20 ft
spread_method: Seeds dispersed by birds and mammals
plants.control_tips: Cut canes to ground repeatedly; dig out root crown. Best tackled in late summer before fruiting.
plants.notes: One of the most abundant and recognizable invasive plants in Portland. Provides dense impenetrable thickets that crowd out native vegetation along streams.

plants.control_tips: Cut vines at base ('ivy rings') and remove ground layer. Do not compost berries.
plants.notes: Smothers forest floors and climbs trees, increasing wind-throw risk and shading out native understory.

plants.growth_habit: Evergreen shrub or tree up to 40 ft
plants.control_tips: Remove before berries ripen in fall. Girdle or cut and treat stumps with herbicide.
plants.notes: Common landscape plant that has spread widely into Portland's forest parks. Outcompetes native shrubs in shaded understory.

plants.growth_habit: Fast-growing deciduous shrub up to 15 ft
spread_method: Wind-dispersed seeds (up to 3 million per plant)
plants.control_tips: Remove before seed set. Dig out root crowns; treat cut stumps to prevent resprouting.
plants.notes: Despite attracting butterflies as adults, it provides no larval host benefits and crowds out native willows and alders along rivers.

spread_method: Seeds transported by shoes, wildlife, water
plants.control_tips: Hand-pull before seed set in spring. Bag and trash — do not compost. Monitor for several years.
plants.notes: Produces allelopathic chemicals that disrupt native plant root fungi. A high-priority early detection target in Portland.

plants.control_tips: Wear gloves — sap and berries are toxic. Pull or dig out before fruiting. Bag all parts and trash.
plants.notes: Extremely toxic to humans and pets. Thrives in dense forest shade where few other invasives survive.

plants.growth_habit: Low perennial from underground corms, 12–18 in
plants.control_tips: Carefully dig out corms; expect multiple seasons of effort. Do not compost any plant parts. Wear gloves — all parts are irritating.
plants.notes: Spreads aggressively from gardens into natural areas. Corms buried deep make eradication difficult.

plants.growth_habit: Tall, bamboo-like stems up to 10 ft; dies back in winter
spread_method: Stem and root material in flood debris
plants.control_tips: Repeated cutting weakens plants over time. Herbicide treatment most effective in late summer. Never compost.
plants.notes: Dense rhizome mats can damage infrastructure. Extremely difficult to eradicate once established.

plants.growth_habit: Enormous hollow-stemmed plant up to 14 ft
plants.control_tips: DO NOT TOUCH — sap causes severe chemical burns and permanent scarring when exposed to sunlight. Contact Portland Environmental Services immediately.
plants.notes: One of the most dangerous plants in Portland. Phototoxic sap causes third-degree burns. Legal eradication required. Free City removal assistance available.

plants.growth_habit: Rapidly climbing vine, up to 1 ft of growth per day in peak season
spread_method: Vegetative spread via rhizomes and stolons
plants.control_tips: Report to City immediately. Repeated cutting, grazing, and herbicide treatment required over multiple years.
plants.notes: Known as 'the vine that ate the South.' Rare but established in a few Portland locations. Early eradication is critical before it spreads.

plants.growth_habit: Dense, spiny evergreen shrub up to 10 ft
spread_method: Seeds viable in soil for 30+ years
plants.control_tips: Cut and treat stumps with herbicide. Highly flammable — significant wildfire risk. Never burn to control.
plants.notes: Extremely flammable and forms impenetrable thorny thickets. Seeds persist in soil for decades. Wildfire risk amplifier.

plants.growth_habit: Tall biennial up to 8 ft with large spiny leaves
plants.control_tips: Remove rosettes in first year before bolting. Cut flower heads before seed set. Wear heavy gloves and protective clothing.
plants.notes: Aggressive competitor in open areas. Dense spine coverage makes removal difficult without protective gear.

plants.growth_habit: Tall reed grass up to 15 ft forming dense monocultures
plants.control_tips: Report to City. Herbicide treatment in late summer followed by cutting most effective. Multiple years of treatment required.
plants.notes: The introduced European haplotype outcompetes native reed and wetland plants, drastically reducing wetland biodiversity and waterfowl habitat.

plants.growth_habit: Tall annual up to 6 ft; hollow stems
spread_method: Explosive seed pods launch seeds up to 20 ft
plants.control_tips: Hand-pull before seed set — relatively easy to pull as roots are shallow. Work upstream to downstream. Check site again after first rains.
plants.notes: Spreads explosively along waterways. Being an annual, consistent removal before seed set can achieve eradication over a few seasons.

spread_method: Seeds on clothing, fur, and equipment
plants.control_tips: Hand-pull when soil is moist; ensure full root removal. Clean boots and equipment after visiting infested areas.
plants.notes: Spreads rapidly in shaded forest understories where few other invasive grasses can thrive. A major threat to Portland's forest parks.
